While the developments from GOOGL’s recent quarter support the bull case, as evidenced by the progress made by Gemini, the Apple partnership with respect to Gemini-Siri, acceleration of the Cloud revenue growth, and support from the EU for its Wiz acquisition.

However, to remain conservative, I am revising my price target to the base case scenario. I am, however, upgrading my stock to a Strong Buy from a Buy, given the significant upside from current levels and given that its medium-term to long-term momentum remains very strong.

Risk Factors

The primary risk to my bull case is the factors that I mentioned in my Bear Case scenario in the previous section. These factors pose more risk to my bullish thesis than last time, especially given that the company’s capex spending for FY26 has nearly doubled.

The other risk to my bull case is the potential for YouTube revenue growth to further decelerate. Q4 saw a significant deceleration on a y/y basis. And while this was attributed to the base effect, given that the same period last year saw a surge in election-related spending, it is still a factor, which, in my opinion, investors should monitor. Any further deceleration in the growth of this segment would imply that GOOGL’s AI efforts are not having a positive impact on boosting ads.

Concluding Thoughts

GOOGL had a strong finish to the year, significantly beating both the top and bottom lines. The company’s AI investments are already paying off, lending support to my previous argument that investors should not punish the company’s intent to spend more on its AI infrastructure buildout. Gemini is on a roll, gaining significant traction. Google Cloud once again blew past all expectations, accelerating at a rate not seen before.

Finally, in my view, it is probably time that investors stop worrying about the death of GOOGL’s search business. The likes of AI Mode and AI Overview have turbocharged the Search business, enabling it to set another quarter of record revenue growth.

From a valuation perspective, my hybrid ensemble model suggests significant upside from current levels, even under a bear case scenario. This, combined with a strong momentum, is the reason why I have upgraded my rating on GOOGL from a Buy to a Strong Buy.

GOOGL may have spooked some investors when it announced that it was significantly boosting its capex spend for FY26. To me, this rattling announcement has only created an attractive entry point in a name that is, in my view, already in the pantheon of the AI revolution.

data.title: Nvidia: The Market Wants Proof, Meta Just Gave It
data.description:
Past Coverage And Investment Thesis:

Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) has been more or less consolidating since I last covered the stock in late November, up only 2.4%, and underperforming the market, with the S&P 500 (SP500) up around 4.6% during the same period.

Even prior to my post-earnings Buy, my hold call pre-earnings on November 17th also shows the same pattern, with the stock down around 0.8% since, underperforming the S&P 500, which is up 2.5% during the same period. The stock is also up around 1.8% since my late August buy, also underperforming the S&P 500, up 5.5% during the same period. You get the gist.

While the stock didn’t exactly go out of the market’s favor per se, investors are standing on the sidelines, especially as fears of an AI bubble reenter the conversation. Just look at the MAG 7 performance year-to-date.

While Nvidia is down 0.8%, Apple (AAPL) is down another 2.9%, Meta (META) is down 3.1%, Google (GOOG) is down 3.5%, Tesla (TSLA) is down 8.6%, Amazon (AMZN) is down another 12.8%, and lastly, Microsoft (MSFT) is down 17.9%.

Nvidia stock isn't underperforming because the business is underperforming; quite the opposite. The stock is underperforming in sympathy with the rest, and because the market is demanding proof of monetization from its customers (Microsoft, Amazon, etc.) before it allows Nvidia to move to the next leg. If Nvidia shows that Blackwell demand is not just strong but also supply-constrained through the end of 2026, this consolidation will likely resolve to the upside. But if the AI bubble narrative gains more steam, specifically as China continues to spend a fraction of the U.S.’s spend, while also coming out with superior models, we could see that $180 level tested again.

The announced partnership with Meta, which I’ll get to in a second, reinforces the idea that big tech isn’t just buying chips for a one-time hype cycle. I think Nvidia is the main driver of the entire AI cycle, and I expect the partnership to reflect renewed confidence in the AI trade.

Nvidia And Meta:

On more positive news, the stock is up over 2.3% on Wednesday after the company announced a multi-year partnership with Meta for AI infrastructure. Meta is set to use Nvidia’s Grace CPUs in its data centers, representing the first large-scale Nvidia Grace-only deployment, while large-scale Vera CPUs are expected to be deployed next year. According to Nvidia’s CEO Jensen Huang:

No one deploys AI at Meta’s scale — integrating frontier research with industrial-scale infrastructure to power the world’s largest personalization and recommendation systems for billions of users…Through deep codesign across CPUs, GPUs, networking and software, we are bringing the full NVIDIA platform to Meta’s researchers and engineers as they build the foundation for the next AI frontier.

While other MAG 7 companies are trying to build their own chips to escape the Nvidia tax, Meta just went the opposite direction and doubled down. Meta has committed to deploying millions of Nvidia processors over the next few years, with Blackwell currently ramping up production and early access to Rubin. In its last earnings report, Meta raised its capex guidance to a staggering $115B–$135B for the year, and a huge chunk of that $135B is now essentially earmarked for Nvidia.

This means that Meta isn't just buying chips off the shelf but has engineers working with Nvidia to co-design the data center architecture. And once Meta optimizes its entire software stack (Llama 4, recommendation algorithms, WhatsApp AI as it integrates Nvidia Confidential Computing into WhatsApp to handle private AI processing) or Nvidia's specific hardware and Spectrum-X Networking, it becomes highly difficult and expensive to switch to a competitor such as Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) or Google’s TPUs.

While server share was not something I monitored closely prior to this deal, and since the competition was between Intel (INTC) and AMD as Nvidia was not part of the conversation, I think having Meta on its side gives it an immediate, high-volume entry into the server CPU market, as the partnership effectively turns Nvidia into a full-stack utility for Meta. Every dollar of Meta’s projected $135B capex that goes toward an Nvidia CPU or Networking switch is a dollar taken directly from the pockets of Intel and AMD, with the latter down over 1.5% on Wednesday on the news, while the former is flat.

The roadmap only gets more aggressive from here. While 2026 will be the year for large-scale deployment of Grace CPUs and the ramp-up of Blackwell GPUs, 2027 is the shift to the Vera CPU and Rubin GPU architecture. Vera will feature 88 custom Arm cores and 176 threads, specifically designed for agentic AI, which is the type of AI that doesn't just answer questions but performs tasks across Meta’s apps.

Valuation And What's Next:

Nvidia is not cheap on traditional metrics, and it should not be. So, I think the question is not whether the multiple looks stretched at forward PE at 39.4x and EV/Sales at 20.8x, but whether earnings power continues to expand fast enough to justify it. While PEG remains within the healthy range of 1.04x, below the sector median, I consider the current consolidation phase healthy. Right now, the market is compressing multiples across mega-cap tech broadly, not just Nvidia. The premium investors were willing to pay at the peak of AI hype has normalized, but forward earnings expectations have not collapsed.

The other variable is hyperscaler discipline. The market is watching Microsoft and Amazon closely for any hint of moderation in AI infrastructure spending, but this year isn’t that, as both companies raised their capex for the year and got punished for it. There is also the geopolitical layer, as concerns around China’s ability to train competitive models at lower cost introduce narrative risk, like what we saw happen in the DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) moment early last year. China is demonstrating superior model performance using constrained hardware, and the market is questioning whether peak GPU intensity assumptions are too aggressive.

Technically, the $180 level has emerged as a clear line in the sand, and a decisive break below would likely be sentiment-driven rather than fundamentally driven. My thesis remains intact, and the consolidation we are seeing today reflects skepticism and valuation digestion, while the underlying demand signal, particularly from Meta, reflects strength.

data.title: Celestica: I Was Wrong, Upgrading On Strong Support
data.description:
After a powerful rally between April and November 2025, Celestica's (CLS) share price has increased by five times, from $70 to $350. Since then, the stock has been trading within a relatively [considering its extremely high volatility] narrow corridor between $280 and $350. CLS's current share price is slightly lower compared to the time when I shared my previous bearish thesis. It means that, by form, I can say that my previous bullish call aged well because the share price declined. However, by substance, my bearishness did not age well, as Celestica continued delivering positive fundamental surprises over the past few months. The fact that there were multiple positive earnings revisions means that Celestica's valuation has improved notably. With AI adoption accelerating across the world and Celestica demonstrating its ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds, I think that we are likely to see new positive surprises from the company. All in all, I upgrade CLS to a "Buy."

I Was Wrong About CLS

The company released its latest quarterly earnings on January 28, delivering a staggering 17th dual earnings beat. Having such an impressive streak of delivering positive earnings surprises indicates the management's stellar capability to plan and execute. Moreover, I want to spotlight the fact that out of these 17 quarters, the "mere" one-cent EPS beat happened only once while all other earnings were marked with comfortable bottom line beats. In my previous bearish CLS articles, I did not pay much attention to this fact, and I believe it was my big mistake because this pattern demonstrates that the management certainly prioritizes building relationships with investors that will be full of trust. When we have such management, we can be confident that building wealth for shareholders is their top priority.

From the financial analysis perspective, Q4 earnings were stellar. The company's YoY revenue growth accelerated once again and achieved a staggering 43.6% increase, an absolute record growth pace over the last several years. The more impressive point is that Celestica's Q4 EPS growth by far outpaced the topline increase. The YoY growth from $1.11 to $1.89 means that Celestica's EPS increased by 70%, demonstrating robust operating leverage. Celestica's full-year EPS increased from $3.92 in FY2024 to $6.06 in FY2025, a 55% increase, while revenue grew by approximately 29%. With soaring EPS, Celestica's cash-generating capacity is also rapidly expanding. The company's full-year net cash from operations [CFO] amounted to $660 million, a 39% increase.

Considering Celestica's stellar P&L and cash flow statement performance, it is no wonder that its balance sheet remains rock-solid. The company ended FY2025 with almost $600 million in cash and $777 million in total debt. Total debt represents approximately one-third of the total equity and just 2% of the market cap. The fact that Celestica's leverage is so low means that the company can potentially exercise robust financial flexibility to ramp up investments in new projects or M&A endeavors. This fundamental strength is very important given the fact that the addressable market is rapidly expanding as the global AI infrastructure spending flywheel is accelerating.

All the largest companies investing aggressively in data centers confirmed that they plan to increase AI capex in 2026, which is a massive tailwind for CLS. Investors of these tech giants are getting really nervous about returns from AI capex, but as a picks and shovels company for the AI era, Celestica is certainly a winner in this case. Celestica has a proven track record of being able to capitalize on AI tailwinds, and that is the reason why I believe recent positive earnings revisions are justified.

I have compiled the above table by using data from the "Earnings revisions" tab. As we can see, there were notable upward EPS revisions since my previous article for all three upcoming years. Please do not forget that Celestica delivered positive EPS surprises over the last 17 consecutive quarters, which means that the management will do its best to deliver positive earnings surprises against revised EPS levels as well. In my previous CLS thesis, I doubted the company's ability to maintain aggressive growth that justifies its valuation because there is heavy reliance on AI tailwinds.

And this year's new wave of the global AI capex boost made me reconsider my position about the longevity and power of AI tailwinds for Celestica. The "AI era" word collocation is not merely a punchy, fancy headline anymore, but it really means that we are in a brand-new era. New releases of large language models are becoming more and more powerful, and they made the public doubt the technological moats of superstar software companies like Adobe and Salesforce. Furthermore, the fact that Palantir's quarterly YoY revenue growth has accelerated over the last 11 quarters and achieved a staggering 70% suggests that the number of AI use cases is rapidly growing. With the number of AI use cases rapidly growing and large language models nearing disruption of the massive software business, hyperscalers are highly likely to continue ramping up their data center investments further.

Considering all the positives, I think that Celestica will see a dramatic P/E ratio compression over the next few years. Wall Street analysts expect the multiple to drop from 31 to 22 by the end of next year, which indicates valuation attractiveness. With the extremely high probability of the global AI data center buildout ramping up further and Celestica's unmatched ability to consistently deliver earnings surprises, I believe that the probability of the company achieving aggressive multiple compression is extremely high.

Risks Update

Despite its status as the AI era's picks and shovels company, sentiment around CLS is also quite weak in 2026 so far. The share price is down by 5% YTD despite hyperscalers announcing their aggressive AI capex plans during the Q4 earnings season. This situation might mean that the AI bubble narrative has become too strong and investors are currently extremely fearful about everything that is related to data centers and semiconductors. If this bearish sentiment persists, Celestica will not rally even if we see new aggressive earnings revisions and confident earnings surprises. The stock market is heavily affected by the sentiment over the short term, which means that the share price might go nowhere from current levels.

From the business perspective, I believe that the competition risk related to potential new entrants is a significant one. We discussed that the addressable market is rapidly expanding, while entry barriers appear to be insignificant given Celestica's extremely low capex-to-revenue ratio. Potential new entrants will mean threats to Celestica's market share, which can result in price wars. Given inherently low margins in the industry, potential price wars can lead to significant margin contraction for Celestica, which will undermine shareholders' value.

Bottom Line

To conclude, I upgrade CLS to a "Buy" because in my previous bearish coverage I underestimated the power and longevity of AI tailwinds for the company. Moreover, my previous analyses missed a very important piece of the puzzle - the company's absolutely stellar earnings surprise record, which underscores the management's exceptional ability to plan and execute. With such a solid combination of planning and execution, I think that CLS will certainly achieve a dramatic P/E contraction over the next couple of years, which means that it is actually undervalued at the moment. Finally, Celestica currently trades at $280, which has proved to be a very strong support level over the last few months, meaning that buying this stock now is likely to be very timely.

data.title: Garmin: Fitness And Aviation Are The Stars In The Future (Q4 Review)
data.description:
Introduction

Garmin Ltd. (GRMN) just reported its Q4 results, which surprised analysts and the investment community overall, with a double beat and an aggressive guidance that led to its shares popping over 10%. I wanted to go over the numbers in more detail and give some comments on the outlook.

With this current pop in the share price, GRMN is still underperforming the S&P 500 (SPY) since my last article on it back in October of last year, but I can see this changing if the performance of certain key growth segments continues to surprise to the upside.

By the numbers

Sales for Q4 came in at $2.12B, up 16.5%, and beat analysts’ estimates by around $110m. Let’s take a look at the individual segments to see which ones performed the best. There is one clear leader here, and that is the Fitness revenue segment, which saw a whopping 42% y/y growth for Q4 to $765.8m, due to a strong demand for wearables, with Venu 4 and Forerunner 970 getting praise. The aviation segment continues to perform well, with 16% y/y growth to $274.2m, and the Marine segment grew even more at 18% growth to $296.9m. Auto OEM declined 3% to $160.3m, and the Outdoor segment, which is the company’s now second biggest revenue generator behind fitness, came in flat to slightly down for the quarter to $627.6m vs $629.3m last year.

Looking at Garmin’s profitability for the quarter and the full year, we can see that for Q4 gross margins were more or less maintained (-10bps) at 59.2% and flat for the full year at 58.7%. Gross profit for the quarter increased 17% to $1.258B and 15% to $4.256B for the year. Operating margins saw a boost of around 60bps for Q4 and the full year, coming in at 28.9% and 25.9%, respectively. In dollar terms, operating income for Q4 increased 19% to $614.1m and 18% to $1.876B for the full year. This gives us Q4 GAAP EPS of $2.73 and $2.79 adjusted EPS, which beat analysts’ estimates by a very respectable 40 cents. A 17% surprise. No one seemed to have expected such a strong operational performance from the Fitness segment, which saw its gross margins grow by a respectable 220bps and 400bps in operating margins, as well as Aviation, which saw a 30bps improvement to already very impressive gross margins to 75.6% and 370bps growth in operating margins.

The company has not only grown in volume, but it has also grown in quality products. Products like Venu 4, Bounce 2, and Forerunner 970 prove that Garmin can stand out in an already saturated smartwatch market and keep improving its profitability through innovation and market share gains. It’s very impressive.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. Garmin finished the year with over $2.278B in cash and equivalents and $459m in marketable securities against virtually zero debt, besides some operating lease liabilities. The company has a very strong balance sheet that supports its long-term vision of innovation across its business segments, as well as rewarding its shareholders with more share buybacks and dividend hikes.

Just this morning, the management announced they intend to increase the dividend by 17%, as well as authorize another $500m share buyback program. It’s not the biggest program, but the dividend increase is certainly quite decent. It is worth noting that the company increased its dividend by over 20% in 2025 following a breakout year for the fitness and aviation segments, so it is a little lower compared to that year, but I don’t think anyone expected such a similar pace to continue. Especially because in the past, the dividend hikes were around 2% for ’23 and ’24. The management is very confident in the business going forward and believes that such a performance that we are seeing right now will be the new normal. That is very exciting for shareholders.

On top of these phenomenal results, the management also issued quite an aggressive guidance for 2026, which seemed to have caught analysts off guard once again. The management expects to see sales at around $7.9B for the year, which is much higher than the consensus of $7.66B, and EPS to be around $9.35, also significantly higher than the consensus of around $8.70.

So, the company reported quite an impressive end to the year, with a double beat and an impressive guidance for 2026, leading to the company's share price skyrocketing on the open as much as 17% at one point, and as of writing the update, the company gave back some of those gains and is up around 10% so far today, which is still quite impressive.

I am very impressed with how well the fitness and Aviation segments continue to perform, and I expect them to continue to be the key growth drivers going forward.

Comments on the Outlook

Let’s focus a bit on the wearable’s opportunity with the newly announced partnership with Truemed and why I think this may be quite a massive revenue driver going forward. Everybody loves getting money back. This partnership will essentially allow Garmin to effectively provide a 30% discount to the customers without actually giving a discount to them, which will allow them to maintain their amazing gross and operating margins in the future.

So, how this is going to work is you purchase an eligible Garmin product, like the Fenix 8 Pro, Venu 4, or Forerunner 970, using your personal funds, but not your HAS/FSA card at checkout, and then go submit a survey from Truemed to assess your health and any risk factors or existing conditions, for example, hypertension, obesity, or high stress, all of which would benefit from being monitored. Then, once that is submitted, an independent licensed medical provider will take a look at your application to see if you qualify and will issue you a Letter of Medical Necessity, turning your Garmin purchase into a lot more than just a neat toy. It becomes a medical necessity.

After the approval, the customer can log in to their HAS/FSA provider’s portal, upload the receipt and the Truemed LMN, and you get reimbursed for the full price if you have enough balance in your HSA account. Since that money came from your pre-tax income, most people will essentially see the purchase as having around a 30% discount.

Many customers will most likely upsell and may not necessarily go for the cheapest product, but rather for the top-of-the-line purchase to minimize their effective tax rate. The “Use It or Lose It” FSA deadline for many employees expires by the 31st of December, so the company will most likely continue to see a great boost in sales in Q4 of the following years, since the customers will want to use up their benefits to lower their effective tax rates. In terms of TAM, there are currently over $150B sitting in HAS/FSA accounts. This will most likely lead to a higher average order value while also creating sticky customers, especially if the doctor “prescribes” a Garmin for heart rate or sleep monitoring.

On top of just the wearables opportunity, which is already quite exciting, the management is looking to expand this strategy to include other products in the future, like the high-end cycling power meters and indoor smart trainers that could qualify for medicalization if they are used for weight management and cardiovascular rehab. Subscription revenue could be higher in the future, too, because Garmin’s Connect+ AI coaching subscription would be much easier to sell if the product is already vetted by a doctor to be beneficial for the person’s health.

Aviation is going to be another key growth factor going forward, as it has been recently. Even if it is not that high in total revenues right now, its impressive margins are a standout opportunity in the long run. The company has what I think is a very strong moat in this segment and is only going to make it even stronger with the acquisition of a 75,000 sq ft hangar and office complex in Mesa, Arizona. The management said that this is “instrumental” for expanding certification throughput. In this business, you cannot just go out and sell the product. The FAA and other similar global bodies must first certify everything for every specific plane model, so by having a lot more space to hire more flight-test engineers, GRMN can certify its newer models much quicker than the competition and grow its order backlog significantly by having a first-mover advantage. In the earnings report, the company said that the Brazilian Air Force already selected GRMN’s G5000H cockpit system for UH-60 Black Hawk helicopters, so I expect more of these contracts to materialize in the future.

Should you invest in Garmin?

I think with an improved performance going forward and my desire to continue to diversify away from pure AI data center plays, Garmin seems to be quite a decent addition to my portfolio. Sure, it has AI in some of its products, but it is an addition to their main hardware-centered strategy, so it’s alright. However, am I going to buy it right now? The answer is no. I think it is still a bit too expensive for what it is going to deliver for now; however, if I see that the profitability is improving a lot quicker than I anticipate, I will revisit the company and will not hesitate to add it to my portfolio.

I expect good things from the fitness and aviation segments in the future. I wouldn’t be selling it right now either, as I believe the company is a good investment if you already got in at a much lower price, so enjoy the massive dividend hike in the near future and keep compounding your gains. I will set a price alert below $190, which it was there quite recently, to see if I would like to buy then.

data.title: FLC: Earnings Should Trend Upward As Interest Rates Decline (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered Flaherty & Crumrine Total Return Fund (FLC), I issued a hold rating due to the fund's use of leverage and how it may suppress total returns. However, FLC has continued to outperform the S&P 500 since my last coverage due to its solid portfolio strategy. FLC has now released an updated annual report for 2025, which prompted me to revisit the fund's performance and outlook for the year. While there are certainly some limitations to the fund, FLC can still be an efficient position for investors that want to prioritize a steady stream of income generation while getting direct equity exposure to stocks in the financial sector.

Looking at the performance over the last 12 months, we can see that FLC's share price has increased by about 4.6%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to partially participate in the positive market momentum over the last few quarters. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 12.2% over the same time frame. The fund now offers a starting dividend yield of about 6.9% while issuing payouts on a monthly basis. After reviewing the most recent annual report, I believe that FLC is capable of supporting its current payout rate over the next 12 months.

Although the share price has increased in the last few months, FLC still trades at a reasonable price to NAV valuation. This could present an opportunity for patient investors that are willing to wait for a lower interest rate environment. The fund still utilizes leverage, which increases the risk profile but can work in FLC's favor once the cost of debt declines. So let's start by taking a look at the fund's updated valuation and financial highlights.

Updated Valuation

At the time of my last coverage, FLC traded at a discount to NAV of 6.92%. Following the uptick in share price, FLC now trades at a slightly smaller discount to NAV of 5.75%. For reference, FLC has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.79% over the last five years, which means that the fund now sits very close to its average range. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that FLC continues to trade at a significant discount to NAV relative to its historical ten-year range. Prior to 2022, FLC frequently traded in the premium to NAV territory, which can be an indication that it's a great time to accumulate shares.

To get better clarity on the fund's internal performance, I wanted to review the latest annual report for 2025. This is the most updated report that provides transparency into the earnings strengths and weaknesses. Net investment income amounted to $1.15 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.39 per share. Looking back on prior years, FLC has now generated net investment income that was slightly higher than its two prior years, but still not as high as 2021's net investment income level of $1.50 per share. What I like about FLC's structure is that net investment income covers most of the distribution by itself, which reduces the fund's reliance on net realized gains to bridge any gaps in its earnings.

This strength in net investment income means that the fund can remain a bit more resilient during periods of market declines. So in a scenario where FLC experiences large net realized losses, the positive net investment income can act as a hedge to offset the declines. This dynamic can lead to better NAV growth and resilience over a longer holding period. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $22.18 per share
  • 2022: $17.22 per share
  • 2023: $16.32 per share
  • 2024: $18.57 per share
  • 2025: $18.93 per share

As the NAV maintains its positive momentum, FLC can get closer to its 2021 high of $22.18 per share. Several years of consistent NAV growth is an indication that the underlying strategy is effective and the fund can easily support the distributions that are being paid out.

Strategy Focuses On Income

According to the latest fact sheet, FLC has total net assets of ~$198M that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing assets. The fund may have a listed annual expense ratio of 4.56%, but this is a bit misleading because it includes expenses related to interest and other operating costs. Since FLC is structured as a closed-end fund, it is legally required to lump in these other expenses as part of its calculation. When excluding these other costs, the actual management fee sits around 1.33% of managed assets.

The fund has the primary objective to provide a high current income. In order to achieve this goal, FLC primarily allocates its capital towards preferred stock and other hybrid securities. In fact, these types of assets account for ~74.8% of total assets. Therefore, it's important to note that FLC is not structurally capable of directly participating in the upside movement of the indices. Furthermore, FLC puts a specific focus on financial securities.

For instance, Bank-focused positions account for 54.89% of total assets. This is followed by an allocation to Insurance and Utility securities, which account for 20.37% and 12.03% of assets, respectively. This top-heavy allocation to financial securities can be a strength during favorable conditions but can also be a source of vulnerability if the industry faces any specific headwinds. Additionally, FLC provides some global exposure with a small portion of its assets being allocated to different regions of the world:

  • United States: 71.50%
  • Canada: 8.33%
  • France: 7.09%
  • United Kingdom: 4.91%
  • Spain: 4.33%

FLC is estimated to have exposure to 254 different positions within. However, its top ten positions make up over ~28% of total assets. When looking at the top positions within the fund, we can see that FLC provides direct exposure to some of the highest quality businesses out there. For instance, the top position is currently Société Générale (SCGLY) at 4.04% of assets. This is followed by many other notable names, including MetLife (MET), Citigroup (C), Morgan Stanley (MS), and Wells Fargo (WFC), just to name a few.

Performance Expectations

The benefit of the portfolio strategy is that the focus on income can be a great hedge against periods of market volatility or uncertainty. For instance, market indices have continued to pull back from their highs as capital shifts out of the technology sector. When comparing FLC to the S&P 500 (SPY), we can see that FLC has outperformed on a YTD basis in both share price and total return. So if you are a bit pessimistic about the outlook of the market for 2026, FLC can be a great option to collect income while we wait for volatility to fade.

Conversely, the emphasis on income generation means that FLC may underperform during strong market rallies. We must remember that the fund is mostly made up of preferred stocks, which do not have the same capacity to increase in value as common shares. Additionally, the fund is actively selling off the fund's upside and income as a distribution, which typically results in slower price growth over time.

For instance, FLC's share price has actually declined by 7.1% over the last decade, despite market indices racing to all-time highs. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 91.4% over the same time frame, which severely underperforms SPY's total return of 324.1% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from FLC's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions over time.

Lastly, FLC uses an aggressive amount of leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings over time. The use of leverage currently amounts to 37.2%, which is equivalent to ~$118.5M. In this higher interest rate environment, an aggressive use of leverage can increase the overall risk profile and negatively impact earnings in two ways. Firstly, a higher cost of debt means that operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities becomes thinner. Secondly, leverage can also amplify losses if allocated to securities that do not perform as anticipated.

Although the Fed has initiated several interest rate cuts throughout 2025, the federal funds rate still sits near the higher end of its ten-year range. As we can see below, the higher interest rate environment has suppressed FLC's share price over the last few years. The fund has struggled to regain enough momentum to get back to its pre-pandemic share price. So my assumption is that lower interest rates will help improve the fund's earnings by providing relief to its operating spread and increasing the value of its holdings.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1037 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 6.9%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by 12 months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.2444 per share. Referring back to the same annual report for 2025, we can see that the fund has no problems generating income that outpaces the distributions being paid out to shareholders. For this reason, I believe that FLC will be able to sustain payouts throughout the rest of 2026. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $1.74 per share
  • 2022: ($3.52) per share
  • 2023: $0.19 per share
  • 2024: $3.31 per share
  • 2025: $1.54 per share

With the exception of the 2022 and 2023 period, FLC has done a great job at demonstrating that its portfolio strategy can generate income that outpaces distributions. There are even periods where the fund has successfully generated income that can support several years worth of payouts in a single period, such as 2024. This can lead to large NAV growth and increase the possibility of supplemental distributions being issued to shareholders.

Lastly, the fund may actually issue distributions in a tax-efficient manner. The latest annual distribution breakdown for 2025 indicates that 100% of the distributions were classified as ordinary dividends. However, the breakdown also confirms that 98.6% of distributions were classified as qualified dividend income, which has the most favorable tax consequences for investors. So there's a chance that distributions may have little tax impact for investors, but I would confirm with a tax professional first.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on FLC to a buy at this time. The fund has continued to demonstrate that it is capable of generating income that far exceeds the distributions being paid, which provides steady dividend coverage and can lead to positive NAV growth over a longer holding period. However, the fund is heavily concentrated on securities within the financial industry, which can be a source of risk if the industry faces any specific headwinds. The fund also specifically focuses on preferred securities, which can offer more resilience against periods of market uncertainty and volatility.

data.title: Clarus: It's Too Early To Play This Turnaround (Downgrade)
data.description:
The last few years have been a really difficult time for shareholders of Clarus Corporation (CLAR). The last article that I wrote about the company, in fact the only article that I wrote about it, was published in March of 2022. Back at that time, I said that it wasn't a bad play. But I wasn't terribly excited about it either. Even though I am generally drawn to companies in the outdoor space, I believed that the stock was not cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook. Since then, revenue, profits, and cash flows have all taken a hit. This has caused the stock to drop 83.7% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 49.9%.

Valuing the company is difficult at this stage. And that's simply because profits and most cash flow metrics are negative. We have seen a bit of a recovery when it comes to domestic operations. But on the whole, the company is clearly struggling. There is the hope that some asset sales might create value for shareholders. But with management even unwilling to project what the near-term outlook might be, I would say that it would be wise for investors to tread very cautiously. I would go so far as to say that until we see strong signs of a turnaround, rating the company a "Sell" is the right choice.

This play has issues

According to the management team at Clarus Corporation, the company operates as a designer, developer, producer, and distributor of outdoor equipment and lifestyle products. Its primary objective is to cater to outdoor enthusiasts. Although I am not personally all that much of an outdoors person, I used to be. I know plenty of people who are. And as somebody who spent a lot of my youth in those types of conditions, this kind of company creates a bit of nostalgia for me.

To really understand the company, we should touch on exactly what it does. For starters, the business has its outdoor business, which consists of Black Diamond Equipment. This operates as a player in the climbing, skiing, and mountain sports equipment space. The products that it produces are geared toward helping those in these outdoor activities push the limits. And the company clearly does this well. In fact, over the span of five years, the company has received over 500 industry awards, with over 250 of those awarded in 2024 alone. Under the adventure category, it has Rhino-Rack, which operates in the aftermarket automotive roof rack and accessories market. There's also MAXTRAX, which produces goods dedicated to helping recover vehicles in harsh terrains. TRED is another offering under this category, with its products including storage boxes, recovery boards and mounts, ramps and wheel chocks, and more. Under the Precision Sport category, we have Sierra, which produces bullets and ammunition. And Barnes Bullets plays in the all-copper bullet technology and innovation space.

The markets that the company plays in are large and expanding. For instance, the outdoor gear and equipment market was worth an estimated $42.2 billion in 2021. By 2028, it should grow to $75.3 billion. That's an annualized growth rate of 5.8%. The gym climbing market should expand from $2.8 billion back in 2022 to $6.9 billion by 2031. And then we have the core climbing market. This is rather small by comparison. It should be worth about $1.6 billion this year. But back in 2020, it was worth only $0.5 billion. There's also the caravan and camping market. Management said that this space was worth about $42.3 billion in 2021. But by 2031, it should grow to $87.6 billion. Meanwhile, by 2028, the global roof rack and hitch market should hit $3.1 billion in size. That compares to the $1.7 billion it was worth back in 2021.

You would think that, with markets like these, Clarus Corporation would achieve attractive and consistent growth. But the opposite has occurred. Over the last three completed fiscal years, revenue fell from $315.3 million to $264.3 million. According to the company, this contraction in revenue largely stemmed from weakness in key markets. Even though the company made certain purchases, such as acquiring TRED in late 2023, soft consumer demand and changes in the outdoor activity market have negatively affected it. During the COVID-19 pandemic, there was a surge in demand for social distancing. This actually worked out quite well for the industries that this business operates in. But after the pandemic came to a close, a return to more normal levels occurred. Naturally, this would cause certain profitability metrics to come under pressure as well. And sure enough, the chart above illustrates that to be the case.

Unfortunately, 2025 was not much better. In fact, during the first nine months of the year, revenue for the company contracted to $185 million compared to the $192.9 million reported the previous year. As the chart below illustrates, actual domestic sales for the company ticked up slightly. But international sales remain weak. It also didn't help that the firm sold off certain assets, including PIEPS, which fell under its Outdoor segment. This was a small sale, only bringing in $9.1 million to the business. But still, for a company this small, even that can have an impact on top-line results. The unfortunate reality is that profitability and cash flows remain under pressure, though some of the cash flow metrics for the company did improve year over year.

I wish I could say that the company made for a good turnaround prospect. But I don't feel like that's the case yet. This doesn't mean that the business doesn't have the potential to turn itself around. In fact, management is actively working on reinventing the business through certain strategic restructuring activities. This includes simplifying some of its operations, such as through a rationalization of its SKUs, and increasing prices to combat tear for later costs. Even so, the company still faces challenges. Consumer confidence levels are near all-time lows. And in 2026, management expects the full unmitigated impact from tariffs to be $3.2 million. However, management is working to offset that and will likely have to do so by cutting costs and hiking prices.

Because management withdrew operating guidance for 2025 back in the first quarter of last year, we really don't know what to expect for the year in its entirety or for 2026. And since the business generates negative cash flow right now, we can't really value it using traditional cash flow or profit figures. What we do know is that, as the chart above illustrates, other companies that have some similarities to it tend to trade at price-to-operating cash flow multiples of between 7 and 32. Meanwhile, the EV to EBITDA multiples I've said companies range between 9.3 and 18.9. Given the poor track record of Clarus Corporation, I would say that lower multiples are justified. But to provide a good range, I created the chart below. In it, you can see how much in operating cash flow that the business would need to generate to trade at either 5, 10, or 15 times adjusted operating cash flow. You can also see the same thing for EBITDA using the EV to EBITDA approach. At the high end in terms of trading multiples, these are not insane figures. But given the overall trajectory of the company in recent years, we have no reason to believe that management will come through on anything like this.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I would argue that while Clarus Corporation is an interesting prospect, it's not one that I'm confident in at this moment. The stock has plummeted in recent years because of weak financial performance. And while there is the prospect of it turning around, we need to see real progress on that before we get optimistic. The one thing that the company does have going for it is that it has a net cash position of $27.5 million. That's quite large for such a small business. It definitely affords the company some optionality. But that's not enough to justify anything other than a "Sell" rating at this time.
